title: Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: Across countries in 2023, the average vegetable oil supply per person plotted against gross domestic product per capita reveals a positive association where higher income levels generally coincide with greater oil availability. The scatter plot places GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis ranging from $2,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis measures vegetable oil supply in kilograms per year per capita from 0 to 35 kg. Data points are color-coded by continent, showing distinct regional clusters. High-income nations in North America and Europe occupy the upper-right quadrant; Canada and the United States sit near the 25 kg mark, while Austria, France, and Ireland cluster between 15 kg and 20 kg at GDP levels exceeding $40,000. In contrast, low-income countries in Africa and Asia appear in the lower-left section, with the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Niger, and Burkina Faso recording supplies below 5 kg at GDP levels under $3,000. Several outliers deviate from the general upward trend. Brazil and Algeria maintain supplies around 25 kg despite GDPs near $20,000, placing them well above the global average for their income bracket. Conversely, Denmark and Norway show lower-than-expected supplies of roughly 5 kg to 10 kg despite having GDPs exceeding $60,000. Middle-income nations like Jordan, Belize, and Suriname occupy the central band with supplies between 10 kg and 15 kg. The distribution shows that while vegetable oil supply increases with economic output, the rate of increase varies significantly across different regions and specific national contexts.
